1) Effect of Universal T ransformer Architecture: To assess the
effectiveness of the proposed method in the VPT dataset,
we used both the UTD-PTP and SD-PTP methods on six
subdatasets of VPT. Each method underwent 90 training
iterations on the six subdatasets of VPT. Throughout the entire
training process, the ADE and FDE results of the models
were recorded every ten epochs, as illustrated in Fig. 4. The
experimental results indicate that compared with the SD-PTP
method, UTD-PTP exhibits notably superior ADE and FDE
performance. This experimental outcome highlights the advan-
tage of the proposed UTD-PTP with a universal transformer
architecture over the SD-PTP method.
2) Effect of Sample Number: We also conducted experi-
ments on the sampling times during the inference process.
As depicted in Fig. 3, we recorded the model performance for
the same model with sampling times ranging from 10 to 70.
Increasing the sampling time can enhance the model’s per-
formance. However, it is noteworthy that the increase in
sampling time also results in a prolonged inference time for the
model, which is undesirable in autonomous driving scenarios.
The determination of a reasonable sampling time involves a
delicate balance between model inference time and prediction
accuracy. Consistent with the established methodology [25],
this article advocates judiciously selecting a sampling time
value of 20. As shown in Fig. 3 on BIT, increasing the sam-
pling time from 10 to 20 significantly reduces the FDE from
5.21 to 4.35, a decrease of 0.86. However, as the sampling
tim
